question_title: Will the interest in “kyrsten sinema” change between 2026-03-19 and 2026-03-27 according to Google Trends?
summary: With a start value of 2 on March 19 (post-spike baseline after the affair story peaked at 100 on March 14), the threshold arithmetic makes this heavily asymmetric. "Decreases" is mathematically impossible since it would require a negative Google Trends value. "Doesn't change" covers the entire baseline range (0-5), which is where this term naturally sits. "Increases" requires a value ≥ 6, meaning a significant new catalyst within the 8-day window. The affair/lawsuit story has fully played out in the media cycle, no court hearings are scheduled, and rulings on the motion to dismiss typically take weeks/months. The ~8% allocated to Increases captures residual risk from unexpected developments.

factors.description: Decreases is mathematically impossible - start value is 2, would need end value < -1, but Google Trends floor is 0
Post-spike decay complete - value has returned to baseline (2) from peak of 100. Spike story (affair admission) is 5 days old and fully played out in media cycle
Full-series base rate: 72.7% no-change rate, but at baseline value of 2 it's even higher since decreases are impossible
Ongoing lawsuit means there could be a new filing, ruling, or development that re-triggers interest. Motion to dismiss was just filed, but rulings typically take weeks/months
No scheduled court hearings or known events in the Mar 19-27 window. Sinema is a former senator with limited ongoing public role
SerpAPI vs pytrends measurement uncertainty at low values (±1-2 points) cannot flip outcome since Decreases is impossible and DC covers 0-5

reasoning:
**Forecast: "Doesn't change" at 92%, "Increases" at 8%, "Decreases" at 0%**

**Key reasoning:**

1. **Threshold arithmetic is decisive.** The start value on Mar 19 is **2** (fully decayed from the Mar 14 spike). "Decreases" would require a value ≤ -1, which is impossible since Google Trends values can't go below 0. "Doesn't change" covers values 0-5, the entire baseline noise range.

2. **The spike has fully decayed.** The affair/bodyguard story broke Mar 13-14 (peaking at 100), then decayed rapidly: 100 → 34 → 9 → 3 → 2 → 2. By Mar 19, interest has returned to baseline. The news cycle is complete.

3. **No catalysts in the forecast window.** No scheduled court hearings, rulings, or events are expected in the 8-day window. The motion to dismiss was just filed and rulings typically take weeks to months.

4. **8% residual risk for Increases** accounts for unexpected developments (surprise ruling, new filings with newsworthy details, Sinema making unrelated news).
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=kyrsten%20sinema&date=2026-02-25%202026-03-27](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=kyrsten%20sinema&date=2026-02-25%202026-03-27) for 2026-03-27 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-19. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "kyrsten sinema",
  "date": "2026-02-25 2026-03-27",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
